nameMeanings.name: ap
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Hungarian Geza, Jeno, Arpad, Attila, Csaba.

Hungarian: variant of   Papp  , meaning ‘priest (of the Catholic or Orthodox Church)’. This surname is also found in Romania, Slovakia, Croatia, and Serbia, and also among Rusyns (in Serbia and Croatia). See also   Pop   2 and   Popa  .
English: variant of   Pape   1.

variants.name: Papp
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Hungarian Laszlo, Zoltan, Geza, Istvan, Gabor, Miklos, Tibor, Arpad, Denes, Imre, Attila.
German (Bavaria, Tyrol): from Middle High German pap ‘baby food’, Dutch pap ‘porridge, pap’, hence perhaps a nickname denoting someone who had lost his teeth or subsisted on a meager diet.

Hungarian: from pap ‘priest (of the Catholic or Orthodox Church)’, probably an occupational name for someone in the service of a priest or a nickname for a pious person. This surname is also found in Romania and Slovakia, and also among Rusyns. Compare Pap and Pop .

nameMeanings.name: Clement
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Alcide, Alphonse, Andre, Marcel, Armand, Cecile, Christophe, Gaston, Olivier, Aime.

English, Catalan, German, Flemish, and Dutch; French and Walloon (Clément): from the Latin personal name Clemens meaning ‘merciful’ (genitive Clementis). This achieved popularity firstly through having been borne by an early Christian saint who was a disciple of Saint Paul, and later because it was selected as a symbolic name by a number of early popes. There has also been some confusion with the personal name Clemence (from Latin Clementia, meaning ‘mercy’, an abstract noun derived from the adjective; in part a masculine name from Latin Clementius, a later derivative of Clemens). In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, especially Italian Clemente , and also their derivatives.

variants.name: Clem
meanings: English (Somerset): from a short form of the personal name Clement or of the female form, Clemence.
Americanized form of German Klemm and German or Dutch Klem: from a short form of the personal name Klemens .

variants.name: Clements
meanings: English, Irish (Tyrone and Antrim), and Dutch: variant of Clement , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s. In North America, this surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, e.g. German Clementz and Slovenian patronymic Klemenc (see Klements ).

variants.name: Clemente
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Carlos, Elia, Manuel, Cesar, Fernando, Luis, Rafael, Alfredo, Andres, Armando, Gerardo.  Italian Antonio, Carmine, Salvatore, Vito, Gino, Angelo, Nicola, Rocco, Enrico, Guido, Marco, Matteo.  Portuguese Joaquim.
Spanish, Italian, and Portuguese: from the personal nameClemente (see Clement ).

variants.name: Crimi
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Angelo, Gaetano, Vita, Cosimo, Giuseppe, Luciano, Luigi, Sal, Tommaso.
Italian (Sicily): from an altered form of the medieval Greek personal name Klēmēs, from Latin Clemens (see Clement ).

variants.name: Chimenti
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Vito, Angelo, Francesco, Filomena, Guido.
Italian (southern): variant of Clementi (see Clement ).

variants.name: Clemon
meanings: English: probably from a variant of the personal name   Clement  .
Perhaps a shortened form of Scottish and northern Irish   McClymont  .

variants.name: Klehm
meanings: North German: from a short form of the personal name Klement (see Clement ).

variants.name: Clementson
meanings: English: from the personal name Clement + -son. See also Clements and Clemson .
Americanized form of Swedish Klementsson or Clementsson: patronymic from the personal name Klement, Clement .
Americanized form of Danish and Norwegian Clementsen or Klementsen, cognates of 2 above.

variants.name: Kelemen
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Hungarian Andras, Gabor, Geza, Imre, Laszlo, Tibor, Bela, Gyula, Istvan, Jozsef, Karoly, Leszlo.
Hungarian, Croatian, Slovak, Rusyn, and Slovenian: from the personal name Kelemen, Hungarian form of Clement . The Hungarian surname is also common in Romania.

variants.name: Climie
meanings: Scottish: from a Scots pet form of the personal name   Clement  .

nameMeanings.name: Gryffudd
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From Old Welsh Grip(p)iud, from an unexplained first element + a second element meaning ‘lord, prince’. Gruffydd ap Llewelyn ( d. 1063 ) was one of the most able rulers of Wales in the Middle Ages, scoring notable victories over the English until he was eventually defeated by King Harold in 1063 .

variants.name: Griffudd
meanings: From Old Welsh Grip(p)iud, from an unexplained first element + a second element meaning ‘lord, prince’. Gruffydd ap Llewelyn ( d. 1063 ) was one of the most able rulers of Wales in the Middle Ages, scoring notable victories over the English until he was eventually defeated by King Harold in 1063 .

variants.name: Gruffud
meanings: From Old Welsh Grip(p)iud, from an unexplained first element + a second element meaning ‘lord, prince’. Gruffydd ap Llewelyn ( d. 1063 ) was one of the most able rulers of Wales in the Middle Ages, scoring notable victories over the English until he was eventually defeated by King Harold in 1063 .

variants.name: Griffith
meanings: Anglicized form of   Gruffudd .

en.lifeSummary: Mrs. Gryffudd ap Clement was born about 1327, in Cardiganshire, Wales. She married Gruffudd Ap Clement about 1347, in Wales. They were the parents of at least 1 son.
